title: Home Health Care Services

narrative.executive_view: Home health has durable paid demand, but it is not a high-AI-labor-replacement roll-up. The six-digit code covers skilled nursing and a broad mix of personal, therapy and support services delivered in the home [S1]. A buyer can improve the documentation, intake, billing and scheduling wrapper, yet the large aide and clinician wage base is care delivered in person. The investable question is therefore disciplined, payer-and-service-line-specific operational improvement rather than an AI-driven labor reset.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The credible AI surface is administrative and clinical-support work: referral completeness, eligibility, revenue-cycle checks, scheduling, documentation drafts and quality review. An older home-health survey reported AI/ML use in revenue-cycle tasks, while the Alliance's 2026 submission identifies EMR integration, cost, workforce readiness, legal uncertainty and home-setting privacy as adoption constraints [S4][S5]. AI should be treated as supervised assistance; it does not substitute for an aide's physical care, a nurse's home visit, or clinical accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Medicare FFS skilled home health is paid through standardized 30-day bundled periods, so an efficient operator can initially retain lower administrative cost. That spread is not fully durable: CMS finalized both a -1.023% permanent PDGM behavioral adjustment and a -3.0% temporary 2026 adjustment [S2]. MedPAC also found a 24.1% average FFS Medicare margin for home-health stays in 2023 [S3]. Medicaid personal care and MA contract economics differ, and state wage-pass-through or plan repricing can transfer savings away from the agency; the selected capture inputs therefore remain conservative.
narrative.adoption_timing: Effective use should cross 50% of a well-run regional agency's administrative wrapper in roughly four years, not immediately. Scheduling and digital workflow familiarity are improving—49.3% of surveyed caregivers said technology helped most with scheduling and shift management [S5]—but home-based workflows, protected health information, model validation and EHR integration slow deployment [S4]. This estimate concerns useful operational adoption, not replacement of direct-care visits.
narrative.consolidation_economics: There is a genuine buyer universe but it is uneven by service line and compliance profile. Mertz Taggart recorded 105 home-based-care transactions in 2025, though only three home-health deals in Q4; it also described sponsor-backed acquirers pursuing add-ons [S6]. The available sub-sector benchmark puts Medicare-certified skilled home health at 4.5x–8x EBITDA, whereas broader home care differs materially by payer and service mix [S7]. Underwriting should normalize owner compensation, referral concentration, EVV and compliance costs, and isolate skilled, private-duty and HCBS economics.
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is durable because AI cannot independently provide the hands-on home services inside this NAICS scope. Medicare coverage is for homebound beneficiaries needing intermittent skilled nursing or therapy, and the current federal payment system still purchases a bundle of in-home clinical services [S1][S2]. That does not make every agency durable: MA network position, local labor supply, referral relationships and rate-setting determine who gets paid and at what margin. The durable claim is about demand for human-delivered home care, not durable retention of every AI saving.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The code is heterogeneous: Medicare-certified skilled care, Medicaid HCBS/personal care and private-pay home care have different payers, margins, licensing and buyers. The supplied 4-digit occupational mix is broader than a single 6-digit operating model, and the selected target population is a regional Medicare-certified skilled provider with some private-pay diversification. Multiples and owner-age evidence are market benchmarks rather than disclosed transaction comps for the $1–10M EBITDA band; both are low-confidence. CMS rebasing, MA bargaining, staffing shortages and non-transferable referral relationships can overwhelm the modest AI benefit.

sources.title: QCEW NAICS Hierarchy Crosswalk
sources.what_it_supports: Confirms 621610 is Home Health Care Services within ambulatory health care; scope framing is supplemented by the frozen prompt and disclosed as heterogeneous.

sources.title: Calendar Year 2026 Home Health Prospective Payment System Final Rule
sources.what_it_supports: CY 2026 PDGM permanent -1.023% and temporary -3.0% payment adjustments and the rate-rebasing channel.

sources.title: MedPAC March 2026 Report to the Congress, Chapter 14
sources.what_it_supports: 2023 FFS home-health margin evidence, covered-service context, workforce constraints and Medicare Advantage mix risk.

sources.title: Alliance for Care at Home RFI on AI in Health Care
sources.what_it_supports: Home-based-care AI opportunities and material implementation, integration, privacy, workforce and legal barriers.

sources.title: 2025 HHAeXchange Survey on Caregiver Technology and Retention
sources.what_it_supports: Caregiver-reported technology use in scheduling and communication; it is a homecare survey, not a direct measure of AI use in all 621610 agencies.

sources.title: Q4 2025 Home-Based Care M&A Report
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 home-based-care transaction volume, active sponsor-backed buyers and service-line-specific M&A activity.

sources.title: Home Care Business EBITDA Multiples: 2026 Benchmark Report
sources.what_it_supports: Published benchmark range of 4.5x–8x EBITDA for Medicare-certified skilled home health; advisory-source benchmark, not a deal-specific comp set.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621610 ($71,424,773,339) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621610 ($113,350,417,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Home Health and Personal Care Aides
Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $72,294 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621610); class <5: 11,187 firms, avg revenue/firm $242,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,310 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,386 firms, avg revenue/firm $660,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,317 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,828 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,118,957 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $176,795 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 7,049 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,771,668 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $437,923 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,983 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,545,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,666,177 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 765 firms, avg revenue/firm $83,228,652 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,150,127 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,983
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Only administrative, documentation, scheduling and revenue-cycle tasks are meaningfully AI-addressable. Hands-on care and clinical visits dominate the supplied mix; estimates preserve that distinction.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit occupational mix spans service models within 621610.
Fractions measure mature AI task assistance/replacement, not legacy non-AI automation or avoided visits.

breakdown_by_role.role: Home Health and Personal Care Aides
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical personal care and companionship remain human-delivered; limited documentation assistance.
Documentation, assessment preparation and coding support are addressable, not skilled visits.

breakdown_by_role.role: Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Clinical support is principally in-person, with a modest documentation layer.
Hands-on assistance remains the core task.
Therapy visits persist; documentation support is limited.
Scheduling, reporting and QA workflow can be assisted, while escalation and management remain human.
Administrative coordination can be assisted but assessment and family engagement remain human.
General office workflow, intake and billing support are a clean automation surface.
Scheduling, intake and document handling are highly assistable with supervised controls.
Occupational therapy remains delivered physically; only documentation is addressable.
Residual includes mixed recruiting, liaison and administrative work; use a moderate blended estimate.

pi_dist.rationale: Bundled payment can retain efficiency between rate cycles, but documented PDGM payment adjustments and payer negotiation limit pass-through retention.
caveats: Applies to a skilled Medicare-oriented target, not Medicaid HCBS rates or all MA contracts.

pi_moat.rationale: Compliance capability, referral relationships and local recruiting can protect a regional operator, but networks and labor markets limit defensibility.
caveats: No agency-specific referral concentration or payer-contract evidence is available.

t50_years.rationale: Digital workflow familiarity supports implementation, while home-setting privacy, EHR integration and oversight barriers delay broad effective adoption.
caveats: This estimates adoption of useful administrative/clinical-support AI, not autonomous clinical care.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Existing technology use and AI interest indicate meaningful but sub-50% effective deployment at the target-operator level.
caveats: No current representative six-digit survey measures effective AI deployment at $1–10M EBITDA agencies.

historical_analogs.value: EHR and revenue-cycle digitization in home health shows that workflow tools diffuse through compliance-heavy operations gradually; it is an adoption analogue, not evidence that AI replaces direct care.
historical_analogs.rationale: The analogue supports phased workflow adoption under integration and privacy constraints.
caveats: The sources do not establish a historical causal adoption curve for generative AI.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: A fragmented private-agency market plausibly includes many retirement-age founders, but no representative 621610 owner-age statistic was located.
caveats: Use as a weak buyability assumption; validate owner age locally before underwriting.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: The report describes sponsor-backed buyers pursuing add-ons and a continuing deal market, consistent with succession-driven supply but not a quantified shortage.
caveats: This is a market-structure judgment, not a documented nationwide owner-survey result.

active_consolidators.rationale: Count reflects a conservative set of active sponsor-backed and strategic home-based-care buyer platforms rather than all possible buyers.
caveats: Not a transaction-database count and varies by skilled, private-duty and hospice service line.

buy_mult.rationale: Selected below the 4.5x–8x skilled-home-health benchmark midpoint to reflect smaller scale, payer and compliance risk.
caveats: Advisory benchmark; no disclosed $1–10M EBITDA transaction comp is supplied.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest platform-quality uplift is possible with compliance, diversified referrals and payer mix, within the published skilled-home-health range.
caveats: Exit assumes no adverse reimbursement or MA-network deterioration and is not a promised multiple.

pricing_structure.value: Medicare FFS 30-day prospective bundled payment, alongside MA negotiated rates, Medicaid program rates and private-pay fees; capture differs by payer.
pricing_structure.rationale: CMS and MedPAC describe the FFS bundled payment mechanics; other payer channels are disclosed as scope heterogeneity.
caveats: No payer mix for the target EBITDA band is available.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The modest automatable labor fraction and four-year adoption estimate imply a limited, phased uplift rather than a step-change in care labor.

terminal_value.justification: Human-delivered in-home clinical and personal care remains paid demand; the uncertainty is rate and network economics, not consumer substitution by AI.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No unrelated occupation, adoption or deal evidence is used as a direct fact. The 4-digit role-mix and broad home-based-care market sources are explicitly disclosed proxies.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: CMS payment adjustments are modeled in pi_dist as a capture/clawback risk; terminal demand is separately based on human service need.

dataset_mismatch.description: Role mix is 4-digit 6216 while the score targets NAICS 621610; labor share combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts as supplied.
sensitivity: A higher personal-care share reduces AI-replaceable labor.
A skilled-agency mix increases documentation and revenue-cycle opportunity but also reimbursement exposure.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Regional Medicare-certified skilled home health agency with limited private-pay diversification
market_boundary.selection_basis: The FFS payment rules and published skilled-home-health multiple range directly inform the selected underwriting case; other subsegments are materially different and remain caveated.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A slower EHR-integrated rollout reduces the adoption factor and delays realized savings.
Payer rebasing, MA contracting and wage rules can transfer the majority of operational savings away from the operator.
Service-line mix, compliance and referral concentration can move valuation more than the modeled AI uplift.

reviewer_note: All seven listed driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. The AI survey page suggested by the frozen prompt was inaccessible on reopen and was not cited.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=4, A=2.5
